APPROPRIATENESS.justification: Both reviewers agree the paper addresses a relevant NLP problem and uses community-standard evaluation showing substantial empirical gains, but they also concur the contribution is weakened by a small WCR dataset and a primarily feature-engineered, non-neural approach. Given the clear practical improvements and solid integration into existing systems, balanced against limited dataset size and novelty concerns, a score of 4 reflects a good fit for the community while noting the reservations about broader impact and modern baselines.

reviewer_a.justification: The topic—coreference resolution on Wikipedia main-concept linking—is clearly within the scope of a top NLP venue and targets the right audience (coreference/community). However, the methodology relies heavily on hand-designed features and linear SVMs rather than end-to-end learned representations, and the empirical evaluation is limited by a very small WCR dataset (30 articles) and absence of comparisons to recent neural baselines (last 3 years), which weakens its relevance to current readers. The paper’s use of standard coreference metrics and comparisons to older baselines is appropriate, but the limited dataset size and engineering-driven approach make it a borderline fit rather than a strong one.

reviewer_b.justification: This paper addresses a clearly relevant NLP problem (coreference on Wikipedia/main-concept resolution) and demonstrates practical utility by producing a large absolute F1 gain on a focused task (89% F1, +13) and improving end-to-end CoNLL F1 by 4 points when integrated into Dcoref. The methodology and evaluation (SVM classifiers, OntoNotes comparison, CoNLL/MUC/B3/CEAF metrics) match community standards and the work is well-positioned for application-oriented audiences. My only reservation for a top-tier venue is the small WCR dataset and largely feature-engineered approach, which may limit perceived novelty, but the concrete gains and system integration make it a good fit for the conference.

CLARITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): Overall the paper is well organized and the main contributions (a classifier for main-concept resolution, 89% F1 and integration as a high-precision sieve into Dcoref) are stated early and carried through the method and experiments. Notation and methodological descriptions (mentioning Wikipedia/Freebase attributes, hand-designed feature families, and two SVMs for pronominal vs non-pronominal mentions) are generally clear, though the presentation could be tightened around the feature families and the rationale for separating classifiers. Figures, tables and examples are reported as effective and the writing quality is good, with only minor places where terminology and the implications of the small WCR dataset size could be explained more explicitly for readers outside the subfield. | B(4): The paper is well-organized with a clear flow from problem motivation through method and experiments, and its main contributions (the classifier, 89% F1 result, and integration with Dcoref yielding a 4-point CoNLL gain) are stated early. Figures/tables and examples are reported as effective and the writing quality is good, with hyperparameters and related work coverage provided. One minor weakness is that the summary doesn’t explicitly confirm detailed notation discussion (so a few technical sections may require careful reading), but overall the presentation is clear and practical for reproduction.

reviewer_a.justification: Overall the paper is well organized and the main contributions (a classifier for main-concept resolution, 89% F1 and integration as a high-precision sieve into Dcoref) are stated early and carried through the method and experiments. Notation and methodological descriptions (mentioning Wikipedia/Freebase attributes, hand-designed feature families, and two SVMs for pronominal vs non-pronominal mentions) are generally clear, though the presentation could be tightened around the feature families and the rationale for separating classifiers. Figures, tables and examples are reported as effective and the writing quality is good, with only minor places where terminology and the implications of the small WCR dataset size could be explained more explicitly for readers outside the subfield.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper is well-organized with a clear flow from problem motivation through method and experiments, and its main contributions (the classifier, 89% F1 result, and integration with Dcoref yielding a 4-point CoNLL gain) are stated early. Figures/tables and examples are reported as effective and the writing quality is good, with hyperparameters and related work coverage provided. One minor weakness is that the summary doesn’t explicitly confirm detailed notation discussion (so a few technical sections may require careful reading), but overall the presentation is clear and practical for reproduction.

ORIGINALITY.justification: Both reviewers agree the paper makes a meaningful, under-studied contribution by defining the WCR task and releasing a dataset while achieving strong empirical gains and useful integration into an existing coreference system. They differ on whether this constitutes sufficient methodological novelty; the system relies on engineered features and existing knowledge sources rather than presenting a new algorithmic or theoretical advance. Given the clear practical value and reproducible empirical improvements but limited conceptual novelty, a middle score reflects the paper's solid but incremental originality.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper addresses a specific niche—identifying mentions co-referent with a Wikipedia article’s main concept—and clearly separates this task and dataset (WCR) from prior general coreference work. However, the technical approach (hand-crafted feature families, Freebase/Wikipedia attributes, and linear SVMs for pronouns/non-pronouns) is primarily engineering-driven and does not introduce a new modeling paradigm or principled theoretical insight. The reported gains and the sieve-style integration into Dcoref are useful empirical contributions, but they largely combine known techniques rather than offer a non-trivial methodological advance that would surprise an expert.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper defines and tackles a clearly articulated, under-studied task—identifying all mentions coreferent with a Wikipedia article's main concept—and provides a new dataset (WCR) and strong empirical gains (89% F1, +13 absolute over baselines) and a useful integration into Dcoref (+4 CoNLL F1). While the modeling approach (hand-designed feature families, Wikipedia/Freebase attributes, and linear SVMs) is largely an application of existing techniques rather than a novel algorithmic advance, the task formulation and practical systemization of external knowledge sources are meaningfully novel and well differentiated from prior coreference work. From an application-oriented perspective this is a notable, reproducible contribution even if the modeling techniques themselves are incremental.

SUBSTANCE.justification: Both reviewers agree the paper demonstrates clear empirical gains and practical utility (89% F1 and improvements to Dcoref) and that related work and hyperparameters are adequately reported. They also concur on notable limitations—small WCR dataset, reliance on hand-engineered SVMs rather than modern end-to-end models, and limited ablation/sensitivity analyses—so while the work is valuable and well-motivated, it falls short of the comprehensive evaluation and reproducibility aids expected for a top score.

reviewer_a.justification: The experiments provide useful evidence (89% F1 and comparison to several prior systems) and related work is adequately covered, but the evaluation relies on a very small WCR dataset (30 articles) and hand-engineered SVM classifiers rather than end-to-end learned models or more recent neural baselines — a serious gap given current practice. Ablation/sensitivity analyses appear limited (no thorough per-feature or error breakdowns reported), though the paper does state some limitations and reports hyperparameters. Overall the empirical validation is adequate to motivate the approach but lacks the comprehensive analyses and modern baselines I expect as a theory-oriented reviewer.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper presents clear empirical gains (89% F1, +13 F1 over best baseline) and demonstrates practical utility by improving Dcoref by 4 CoNLL F1 points, which aligns with my application-oriented view. It also candidly discusses limitations (small WCR dataset, precision-focused gains) and reports hyperparameters and related work adequately. Missing, however, are stronger reproducibility aids (code not mentioned) and detailed ablation/sensitivity studies of the hand-designed feature families and the two-classifier design, which would have closed the remaining minor gaps.

MEANINGFUL_COMPARISON.justification: Both reviewers agree the paper evaluates against appropriate, influential baselines and uses standard metrics, and they both note candid discussion of dataset limitations and genre mismatch. The main shortcoming is the lack of more recent end-to-end neural baselines and limited analysis given the small WCR corpus, which prevents a perfect score but does not undermine the fairness of the core comparisons. Given these strengths and modest omissions, a score of 4 reflects well-motivated comparisons with some gaps to address.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper compares to several classic and mid-2010s baselines (Dcoref, Clark & Manning 2015, Durrett & Klein 2013/2014, Martschat & Strube 2015) and reports standard metrics (CoNLL F1, MUC, B3, CEAFφ4), which is appropriate. However, it relies on two linear SVMs with hand-designed feature families rather than modern end-to-end neural systems, and the summary does not indicate inclusion of more recent neural baselines (within the last ~3 years) or clear retraining/conditioning of baselines on the small WCR corpus—raising fairness concerns. The paper does acknowledge dataset-size limitations and that news-trained systems perform poorly on Wikipedia, but there is limited evidence of thorough analysis contrasting why the proposed method outperforms prior models beyond integration into Dcoref. Overall the comparisons are reasonable but incomplete by current standards, so I mark it as mixed (score 3).

reviewer_b.justification: The paper compares against appropriate, recent and influential baselines (Dcoref, Clark & Manning, Durrett & Klein, Martschat & Strube) and evaluates using standard metrics (CoNLL F1, MUC, B3, CEAFφ4), which supports fair cross-system comparison. It is candid about limitations (small WCR dataset, genre mismatch) and reports integration gains when adding their classifier to Dcoref, but the small dataset and lack of mention of some contemporary neural baselines leave minor gaps. Overall the comparisons are well-motivated and analyzed, with only modest omissions that prevent a perfect score.

REPLICABILITY.justification: Both reviewers agree the paper reports model details and evaluation metrics but omits critical replication artifacts: release status of the WCR dataset, detailed preprocessing/feature-extraction steps, and code. These gaps make faithful reimplementation nontrivial, so while many components are specified the work is only partially reproducible, warranting a mid-level score.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper provides some necessary details (hyperparameters are reported; evaluation metrics/procedures are standard and precisely stated), but key replication elements are missing or under-specified. The small WCR dataset is described but its public availability is not stated, and the preprocessing pipeline for Wikipedia/Freebase attribute extraction is not clearly detailed. The method relies on families of hand-designed features and two SVMs, yet it is unclear whether feature specifications and engineering heuristics are fully enumerated and code is not mentioned, making faithful reimplementation uncertain. From a strict reproducibility perspective these omissions mean only partial reproducibility.

reviewer_b.justification: Paper reports hyperparameters and model details (linear-kernel SVMs, separate pronominal/non-pronominal classifiers) and specifies evaluation metrics (CoNLL F1, MUC, B3, CEAFφ4). However, the WCR dataset is very small (30 articles) and it is unclear whether it is publicly released or whether the preprocessing pipeline (Wikipedia/Freebase attribute extraction, feature construction families) is fully specified; code is not mentioned. Given these minor but important gaps, the work is mostly reproducible but would require contacting authors or reimplementing some dataset/feature extraction details.

IMPACT.justification: Both reviewers agree the paper addresses a practical and useful problem and demonstrates substantial empirical gains (large F1 improvements and usable boosts when integrated into an existing system), but they also concur that the contribution is limited by reliance on hand-engineered features, lack of comparison to modern neural baselines, and evaluation on a very small WCR corpus which constrains generality. Given these strengths and clear practical utility tempered by limited broader impact and reproducibility concerns, a middle-ground score best reflects its likely modest but useful influence on applied pipelines rather than on core research directions.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper tackles a specific and practical problem (main-concept resolution in Wikipedia) and shows measurable gains (+13 F1 on the WCR task; +4 CoNLL F1 when added as a high-precision sieve), but its impact is constrained by heavy reliance on hand-crafted features and linear SVMs rather than end-to-end learned representations and by evaluation on a very small WCR corpus (~30 articles). It also omits comparisons to recent neural baselines and provides limited evidence that the approach generalizes beyond this niche genre, so the results are unlikely to substantially change researcher or practitioner approaches or to spawn broad follow-on work.

reviewer_b.justification: This paper tackles a practically important problem—resolving mentions of an article's main concept in Wikipedia—which matters for knowledge-base population and downstream IE on web text. The reported 89% F1 (13-point absolute gain over the best baseline) and a +4 CoNLL F1 improvement when integrated as a high-precision sieve into Dcoref show concrete, usable gains that would change how practitioners adapt coreference systems to Wikipedia-like genres. The work is pragmatic (feature-based SVMs, use of Wikipedia/Freebase attributes) and likely to be adopted or extended in applied pipelines, though the small WCR dataset (30 articles) limits immediate generality and exploratory follow-ups will be needed.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: Both reviewers agree the paper makes clear, testable claims and provides empirical evidence showing substantial gains, but they also agree the evaluation is limited by a very small in‑domain dataset and lacks reproducibility details or significance testing. Reviewer A additionally flags methodological conservatism (hand‑crafted features, no recent neural baselines) while Reviewer B finds the methodology reasonable; given these shared empirical limitations and the absence of stronger validation, a cautious middle score of 3 is appropriate to reflect promising results that are not yet fully convincing or broadly validated.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper states clear experimental claims (89% F1 on WCR; +13 F1 over best baseline; +4 CoNLL F1 when added to Dcoref) and supplies empirical evidence, but the evaluation is based on a very small in-domain dataset (30 articles, ~60k tokens) which raises generalization concerns. Methodologically it relies on hand-designed feature families and linear SVMs rather than end-to-end learned representations, and it does not compare to recent neural coreference baselines (within the last ~3 years), both of which I penalize as a theory purist. The paper also appears to lack thorough ablation/failure analyses and public code, so while results seem promising they are not yet fully convincing or broadly validated.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper states clear, testable claims and supports them with experiments against appropriate baselines (Dcoref, Clark & Manning, Durrett & Klein, etc.) using standard CoNLL metrics; the reported 89% F1 and a 13‑point gain for the WCR task and a 4‑point CoNLL improvement when integrating the sieve are plausible given the described SVM + feature approach. Methodology is sound in that genre-specific features, separate pronominal/non‑pronominal classifiers, and comparisons to prior work are provided, and hyperparameters are reported. My main technical concerns are empirical: the WCR dataset is very small (30 articles), which limits claims about generalization and robustness, and no code/reproducibility details or statistical significance testing are mentioned, so some reported gains may not be fully validated. Overall the evidence largely supports the claims but with minor empirical limitations that do not invalidate the core technical contribution.

main_claims: A classifier that accurately identifies all mentions co-referent to the main concept in a Wikipedia article was developed.
The model achieves 89% F1 on the task, an absolute gain of 13 F1 points over the best baseline.
Incorporating the classifier as a high-precision sieve into the Stanford deterministic rule-based system (Dcoref) yields a 4 percentage-point CoNLL F1 improvement on a full coreference task.
_summary.methodology: Compute a representation for each mention and the article's main concept using Wikipedia and Freebase-derived attributes; extract families of hand-designed features and train two linear-kernel SVM classifiers (one for pronominal, one for non-pronominal mentions) to identify mentions co-referent with the main concept.
datasets: WCR (30 Wikipedia articles, ~60k tokens, OntoNotes-style annotations)
limitations: The WCR dataset is far smaller than OntoNotes (dataset size limitation).
State-of-the-art coreference systems trained on news perform poorly on Wikipedia; statistical systems require retraining/adaptation for this genre.
The reported overall gain is larger in precision than in recall.

reviewer_a.name: Theory Purist
reviewer_a.background: Senior computational linguist with a PhD in formal semantics; 15+ years reviewing and publishing on coreference theory and representation learning.
traits: Penalizes papers that rely primarily on hand-crafted features instead of end-to-end learned representations
Requires comparisons to the most recent neural baselines (within last 3 years) for empirical claims
Discounts papers with limited analysis of failure modes or limited ablation studies (expects per-feature and error analyses)
Prefers principled model formulations or theoretical insight; gives lower scores to largely engineering-driven improvements
reviewer_a.reviewing_style: Strict, detail-oriented reviewer who expects principled modeling and comprehensive empirical validation; will downgrade claims that lack modern baselines or theoretical justification.

reviewer_b.name: Application-Oriented Empiricist
reviewer_b.background: Applied NLP researcher with industry and academic experience in information extraction and knowledge base population; frequent reviewer for CoNLL and applied NLP venues.
traits: Rewards clearly defined new tasks/datasets and practical problem formulations that address real-world needs
Gives credit for strong absolute empirical gains and useful integrations with existing systems (even if achieved with feature-based models)
Values reproducibility and system utility: rewards papers that produce measurable improvements when plugged into established pipelines
Less strict about using the latest model class if the approach yields clear, interpretable, and deployable gains
reviewer_b.reviewing_style: Pragmatic reviewer who favors well-motivated tasks and reproducible empirical gains; willing to accept engineering solutions when they demonstrably improve downstream systems.
